fields.narrative_intro: *A terminally-earnest person and a frazzled person discuss the $60B Cursor acquisition at a pop-up ad that won't close.*
fields.panel1:
[setup]
TERMINALLY-EARNEST PERSON: "SpaceX just bought Cursor for sixty billion dollars."
fields.panel2:
[build]
FRAZZLED PERSON: "Cursor — the app developers use to avoid writing code?"
TERMINALLY-EARNEST PERSON: "Twenty times revenue. All-stock."
fields.panel3:
[pivot]
FRAZZLED PERSON: "So he paid $60B to own the tool... that writes the code... for the engineers... he already owns?"
fields.panel4:
[silent reaction]
[Visual: Both characters stare into the middle distance. The uncloseable pop-up ad behind them quietly refreshes to display the Cursor homepage. A tiny rocket drifts past the corner of the screen.]
fields.panel5:
[punch]
TERMINALLY-EARNEST PERSON: "The data. He gets every prompt developers ever type."
FRAZZLED PERSON: "...oh no. He's buying the thoughts."
fields.panel6:
[button]
[Visual: The pop-up ad finally closes — revealing another pop-up that just reads: 'ANYSPHERE HAS BEEN ACQUIRED. YOUR AUTOCOMPLETE NOW BELONGS TO MARS.' Neither character moves.]

narrative_summary: The joke escalates from financial absurdity to existential dread: SpaceX didn't buy a coding tool, it bought the unfiltered inner monologue of every developer on Earth — revealed when the unkillable pop-up ad delivers the news directly.
rationale: Candidate 2 earns its score by escalating to a genuinely fresh and unsettling angle — 'he's buying the thoughts' — that reframes the acquisition as cognitive surveillance rather than a software deal, which is both more surprising and more specific than the training-data observation alone. The button panel ('YOUR AUTOCOMPLETE NOW BELONGS TO MARS') delivers a earned, absurdist closer that doesn't just restate the setup, and the uncloseable pop-up prop does real structural work throughout.
place: at a pop-up ad that won't close
hat_pair: shower cap covered in sticky notes
